file_name: PlayboyEnterprisesInc_20090220_10-QA_EX-10.2_4091580_EX-10.2_Content License Agreement_ Marketing Agreement_ Sales-Purchase Agreement1.txt

perturbation.original_text: In the event Client fails to provide its approval or disapproval of any or all things submitted to Client pursuant to this Section 1 within ***** of Client's receipt thereof, EFS may send written notice to Client advising no response was received. If Client does not respond within ***** of Client's receipt thereof, then Client shall be deemed to have given disapproval.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event Client fails to provide its approval or disapproval of any or all things submitted to Client pursuant to this Section 1 within **thirty (30) days** of Client's receipt thereof, EFS may send written notice to Client advising no response was received. If Client does not respond within **fifteen (15) days** of Client's receipt thereof, then Client shall be deemed to have given disapproval.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies a timeline for Client approval of EFS Produced Merchandise. By changing the response window, it might create a period where the client has less time than what would be considered reasonable under UCC Article 2, specifically 2-309 which discusses reasonable time for actions. Depending on the complexity of the approval needed, this may be too short.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-309 states that when a contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration, it is valid for a reasonable time. Changing the approval time frame to 30 days and then 15 days for non-response could be considered unreasonable, especially for complex approvals, conflicting with the UCC's requirement for a 'reasonable time' for an action.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the approval timeline, potentially making it unreasonable, which contradicts UCC § 2-309. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that if a time for action is not agreed upon, it shall be a reasonable time. By shortening the client's response window, the contract risks violating this 'reasonable time' requirement, especially for complex approvals as described in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. This creates an inconsistency that undermines the legal standard.

perturbation.original_text: Such amount shall be paid Net ninety (90) days following entry of such Client Inventory into EFS' system; provided, however, that EFS shall enter all such Client Inventory upon receipt or shall promptly notify Client in the event it is unable to do so due to missing information (i.e. barcodes) or because any product is incomplete or otherwise in a condition not fit for immediate resale.
perturbation.changed_text: Such amount shall be paid Net **180** days following entry of such Client Inventory into EFS' system; provided, however, that EFS shall enter all such Client Inventory upon receipt or shall promptly notify Client in the event it is unable to do so due to missing information (i.e. barcodes) or because any product is incomplete or otherwise in a condition not fit for immediate resale.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms to Net 180 days extends the payment period, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Illinois law. This is because such an extended payment term can be viewed as commercially unreasonable and exploitative, particularly if it significantly disadvantages the supplier (Client).
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing (Illinois)
perturbation.law_citation: Illinois Law, Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Illinois law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. This covenant requires parties to act with reasonable commercial standards of fair dealing. Extending the payment period to 180 days may be seen as commercially unreasonable and an exploitation of contractual power, especially if it causes financial strain or detriment to Client, thus breaching the implied covenant.

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment period to 180 days, which the perturbation and law explanations suggest could violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Illinois law. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is empty, Snippet 1 indicates the existence of the Illinois Compiled Statutes (ILCS), which provides a framework for laws and regulations in Illinois. The extended payment term could be viewed as commercially unreasonable, potentially causing financial strain and breaching the implied covenant. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where a time-sensitive obligation (payment deadline) does not align with legal requirements, making the terms unenforceable.
